dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a high-priority sick person call, suspected stroke.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I woke up with numbness in my face and trouble speaking, and my left arm was weak.
You're alert. Glasgow Coma Scale is 13-you're oriented to place only.
When did these symptoms start? Did anything trigger them?
The care staff woke me up at 8:20 and noticed the weakness. I don't remember last night well.

dialogue.micro_intent: special tests - Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale
dialogue.utterance: We observed numbness when brushing your left eyebrow, and your left arm drops. These point to a stroke alert. Your Cincinnati Stroke Scale is positive.
We understand you have gout, diabetes, high cholesterol, and high blood pressure. Also, three past strokes.
Are you insulin-dependent for diabetes? Has your blood sugar ever been an issue?
Yes, I've had diabetes and had to use insulin.
The care team mentioned a new bruise on your face-do you recall tripping or falling recently?
I don't think I fell. I feel a little nauseous, but no chest pain or shortness of breath.
Let's check your vitals. Blood pressure is 132 over 87 right now.
Your respiratory rate is 16 breaths per minute, and heart rate is 77.
Let's check your blood sugar. It's important because of your diabetes.
Your blood sugar is low at 56. We're going to give you some oral glucose right away.
Since you can swallow safely, we're giving you 15 grams of oral glucose now.
You might feel a little better soon. You're cool but also diaphoretic. Any less numbness yet?
Your blood pressure is now 147 over 76, heart rate is at 78.
Your respiratory rate is up to 20, oxygen saturation is 99%.
Your blood sugar is now 80. That's an improvement. Does that feel better?
We're going to try placing an IV in your arm or leg for potential medication use.
First IV attempt unsuccessful.
Second IV attempt also unsuccessful in another location.
Third IV attempt also unsuccessful-no usable vein found.
You mentioned trouble speaking. Can you say, "The sky is blue" for us?
The... blur... blue.
We noted left arm droop. That supports a stroke-like presentation.
Your left eye is reactive, but you mentioned blurred vision. Has that been an ongoing problem?
Yes, the blurred vision started a bit ago. The numbness is clearly new.
You also mentioned some nausea. Is it constant or does it come and go?
The latest vitals show 118 over 76, heart rate 72, and respirations at 20.
Your oxygen saturation is at 100% now, and still no chest pain.
We're running a 4-lead ECG now to monitor your heart throughout our care.
A 12-lead EKG was performed as well. It shows sinus rhythm with nonspecific T wave abnormality.
Stroke protocol is now active, we are following medical-stroke tia protocol 3-5 for cerebral infarction.
We've completed all documentation for monitoring and interventions.
Your diabetes, hypertension, and stroke history make this an urgent situation. The goal is rapid transport to their CT suite.
We'll monitor blood sugar, pupils, and motor function regularly on the way in.
You're being secured now with a 5-point harness for safe transport.
Our equipment, patient history, and vitals are ready. En route to St. Mary's Adult Emergency Department.
We'll continue taking vitals every 10 minutes. We will also stay ready for another blood sugar check.
We need to check your blood sugar again before arrival at the hospital.
Your blood sugar has dropped slightly to 71, but otherwise you are stable.
Your condition still includes numbness on the left side and speech difficulty. No change overall detected.
No new bleeding or facial droop, and mental status remains consistent.
We are taking you directly to their stroke alert area as a priority.
The hospital has been alerted to the stroke case and expects us now.
All equipment, vitals, medications, and medical history from care staff have been relayed to the emergency team.
All neurological and vital signs remain unchanged. You'll be moved immediately to imaging.
No EMS delays, and arrival at the facility is imminent.
The team will meet us at the door for direct CT scanner entry.
You're now in the care of St. Mary's emergency team and being transferred for imaging.
